_provenance: Claude-authored original Gaokao reading, batch D2. ~220-280 word passages, 4 MCQs each (detail/inference/main idea/attitude/vocab). Gaokao difficulty. DeepSeek not used.

sets.passage: In 1975, Junko Tabei became the first woman to reach the summit of Mount Everest. Her achievement, remarkable in itself, is made even more so by the obstacles she had to overcome to get there. Growing up in Japan, Tabei loved mountains from childhood, but in those days climbing was considered an activity for men. Many people, including some climbers, openly doubted that a woman, and a small one at that, could survive the world's highest peak. Tabei refused to be discouraged. She formed a women's climbing club, whose motto declared that women could do great things on their own. Money was a constant problem; sponsors were reluctant to support a team of women, so the climbers made their own equipment and paid their own way. On the mountain itself, disaster nearly struck when an avalanche buried Tabei and her companions in their tents. She was knocked unconscious, yet days later she pushed on to the top. Throughout her life, Tabei disliked being called simply 'the first woman' to climb Everest. She preferred to be remembered as the thirty-sixth person to do so, insisting that she had climbed as a person, not merely as a woman. After Everest, she went on to climb the highest peak on every continent. Her story is not only about mountains, but about the quiet determination to ignore those who say a thing cannot be done.

questions.prompt: What did Junko Tabei achieve in 1975?
options: She was the first person ever to climb Everest.
She was the first woman to reach the summit of Everest.
She was the youngest climber on Everest.

questions.prompt: What problem did her climbing team face?
options: Money problems, as sponsors were reluctant to support women.

questions.prompt: How did Tabei prefer to be remembered?
options: As the first woman to climb Everest.
As the 36th person, who climbed as a person, not just a woman.

questions.prompt: What is the main message of the passage?
options: The determination to ignore those who say a thing cannot be done.
That women should not climb mountains.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 首位登顶珠峰的女性)、细节(B 赞助商不愿支持女队、缺钱)、细节(B 愿被记为第36位登顶者、以人而非女性身份)、主旨(B 不顾质疑、坚定去做)。

sets.passage: On the roof of a busy supermarket in a crowded city, rows of vegetables grow under the open sky. This is one of a growing number of 'rooftop farms', and it offers a clever response to several modern problems at once. As cities grow, good farmland disappears, and food must often be transported over long distances, using energy and losing freshness. Rooftop farms turn unused space into productive gardens. The vegetables grown there can be sold or eaten just metres from where they are picked, so they are fresher and require almost no transport. The plants also help to cool the building below, lowering energy bills, and they make use of rainwater that would otherwise run wastefully away. There are challenges, of course. Roofs must be strong enough to bear the weight of soil and water, and not every building is suitable. Setting up such a farm can be expensive, and growing food in a city brings problems of its own, such as pollution. Yet supporters argue that the benefits go beyond vegetables. Rooftop farms bring communities together, teach children where food comes from, and bring a welcome touch of green to grey city landscapes. They will not feed an entire city, but they point towards a future in which the line between the city and the farm is no longer so sharp.

questions.prompt: What grows on the supermarket roof?
What is one benefit of rooftop farms?
options: They make the building much heavier.
Fresher food, less transport, and a cooler building below.
They use a great deal of energy.
They block out all the sunlight.

questions.prompt: What is one challenge of rooftop farms?
options: There is no sunlight on roofs.
Roofs must be strong enough, and setting them up is expensive.
It is always too cold on roofs.

questions.prompt: What future do rooftop farms point towards?
options: A future where the line between city and farm is no longer so sharp.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 蔬菜)、细节(B 更新鲜、少运输、给楼降温)、细节(B 屋顶需够结实、造价高)、主旨/推断(B 城与乡的界线将不再那么分明)。

sets.passage: Should museums be free? In recent years, a growing number of countries have decided that they should, removing entrance fees from their national museums. The results of this experiment offer interesting lessons about the value of culture. Those who support free entry argue that museums hold the treasures of human history and art, which belong to everyone, not just to those who can afford a ticket. When entry is free, they say, museums are visited by a far wider range of people, including families, students and the poor, who might otherwise never come. Culture, in this view, is not a luxury but a right. Critics, however, raise practical concerns. Museums are expensive to run, and without ticket money, they must depend on the government or on donations, which can be uncertain. Some worry that 'free' museums are not really free at all, since they are paid for by taxes that everyone, including non-visitors, must contribute. The evidence so far is mixed but encouraging. In many places, free entry has indeed brought a sharp rise in visitors, especially among young people and those visiting for the first time. Whether this is worth the cost is, in the end, a question not of economics alone but of what kind of society we wish to be. A nation that opens its museums to all is making a quiet statement: that its culture is a shared treasure, meant to enrich every citizen's life.

questions.prompt: What have a growing number of countries done?
options: Removed entrance fees from their national museums.

questions.prompt: What do supporters of free entry argue?
options: That culture belongs to everyone and is a right, not a luxury.
That only the rich should visit.

questions.prompt: What concern do critics raise?
options: That museums hold too few treasures.
That museums are costly and must depend on uncertain funding.
That having too many visitors is bad.

questions.prompt: What 'quiet statement' does a nation with free museums make?
options: That its culture is a shared treasure meant to enrich every citizen.
That its taxes are too high.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 取消国家博物馆门票)、细节(B 文化属于所有人、是权利而非奢侈)、细节(B 运营贵、依赖不稳定资金)、主旨/推断(B 文化是惠及每位公民的共同财富)。

sets.passage: For a long time, humans liked to believe that we alone were truly intelligent. The more scientists study animals, however, the more this belief is challenged. Among the most surprising discoveries is the remarkable cleverness of crows. Crows, it turns out, can do things once thought impossible for a bird. They can use and even make tools, bending wire into hooks to reach food. In experiments, they have solved complex puzzles that require several steps in the right order. Most astonishing of all, they appear to plan for the future and to recognise individual human faces, remembering for years a person who once treated them badly. This intelligence serves them well in a world increasingly shaped by humans. While many animals struggle as cities spread, crows have learned to thrive among us, finding food and even using traffic to crack open nuts. Their success is a sign not of luck, but of a flexible, problem-solving mind. The study of crow intelligence does more than amaze us. It forces us to rethink what intelligence is and where it can be found. Cleverness, it seems, is not the special possession of humans, or even of large-brained mammals, but something that has appeared, in different forms, across the animal world. Perhaps the wisest response is a little more humility about our place in nature.

questions.prompt: What surprising thing can crows do?
options: Use and make tools, and solve complex puzzles.

questions.prompt: How do crows thrive in human cities?
options: They find food and even use traffic to crack open nuts.
They sleep through the whole day.

questions.prompt: What does the study of crows force us to do?
options: Rethink what intelligence is and where it is found.

questions.prompt: What response does the writer suggest?
options: A little more humility about our place in nature.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 会用/造工具、解多步谜题)、细节(B 觅食、借车流压坚果)、推断(B 迫使我们重思智能的定义与所在)、主旨/态度(B 对人类在自然中的位置多些谦卑)。

sets.passage: A new kind of holiday has become popular in recent years: 'voluntourism', in which travellers spend part of their trip volunteering, perhaps teaching children, building houses, or helping at animal shelters in poorer countries. For many young people, it seems the perfect way to see the world while doing good. But the reality is more complicated than the bright advertisements suggest. At its best, voluntourism can bring real benefits. It can provide help where it is needed, raise money for good causes, and give travellers a deeper understanding of other cultures than ordinary tourism allows. Many volunteers return home changed, more aware of the wider world. However, critics point to serious problems. Volunteers often stay only a short time and lack the skills to be truly useful; a house built badly by untrained tourists may do more harm than good. Worse, the constant flow of short-term volunteers can create unhealthy dependence, and in some cases children's homes have even been kept open mainly to attract paying visitors. The lesson is not that we should stop trying to help, but that good intentions are not enough. Real help requires listening to local people about what they actually need, supporting their own efforts rather than replacing them, and being honest about who truly benefits. To help well is harder than it looks, and it begins with humility rather than with a plane ticket.

questions.prompt: What is 'voluntourism'?
options: Travel that combines tourism with volunteering.

questions.prompt: What benefit can voluntourism bring?
options: Real help, money for good causes, and deeper cultural understanding.

questions.prompt: What problem do critics point to?
options: Short stays, a lack of skills, and unhealthy dependence.

questions.prompt: What is the real lesson of the passage?
options: We should never try to help others.
Good intentions are not enough; real help needs humility and listening.
Volunteers should simply be paid more.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 旅游+志愿结合)、细节(B 提供帮助、筹款、加深文化理解)、细节(B 短期、缺技能、造成依赖)、主旨/推断(B 善意不够、需谦卑与倾听)。

sets.passage: Vincent van Gogh is today one of the most famous and beloved painters in history. His vivid pictures of sunflowers and starry skies sell for enormous sums and are admired by millions. Yet during his own lifetime, van Gogh was a failure by almost every measure, and his story holds a powerful lesson about the meaning of success. Van Gogh did not begin painting seriously until he was nearly thirty, and he taught himself largely through endless practice. He worked with astonishing energy, producing about two thousand artworks in just over a decade. But almost no one wanted them. He sold, by most accounts, only a single painting in his entire life. Poor, often hungry, and troubled by illness, he depended on his brother for support and was frequently in despair. What kept him going was not the hope of fame or money, neither of which he ever enjoyed, but a deep, private need to express what he saw and felt. He painted because he had to, not because the world rewarded him for it. Only after his death did the world begin to understand his genius. His story is often told as a tragedy, and in many ways it was. But it also raises a quieter question: van Gogh created beauty that has moved millions, asking nothing in return. Was his life, then, really a failure, or do we simply measure success by the wrong things?

questions.prompt: How successful was van Gogh during his own lifetime?
options: A failure who sold only one painting.

questions.prompt: What kept van Gogh painting?
options: A deep, private need to express what he saw and felt.

questions.prompt: When did the world begin to understand his genius?
What question does his story finally raise?
options: Whether we measure success by the wrong things.
Whether brothers should help each other.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 一生只卖出一幅、近乎失败)、细节(B 出于表达所见所感的内在需要)、细节(B 死后世人才懂其天才)、主旨/推断(B 是否我们衡量成功的标准错了)。

sets.passage: We spend about a third of our lives asleep, yet for a long time science treated sleep as little more than a switching-off of the body, a passive pause between the important hours of waking life. Recent research tells a very different and far more interesting story. Far from doing nothing, the sleeping brain is remarkably busy. During the night, it sorts through the experiences of the day, strengthening important memories and clearing away what is unneeded. This is why a good night's sleep before an exam can be more useful than a night spent studying: the tired brain that has not slept simply cannot store information well. Sleep also cleans and repairs the body. A special system, most active during deep sleep, removes waste products that build up in the brain during the day. Too little sleep, repeated over time, has been linked not only to poor concentration but to weakened immunity, weight gain, and a higher risk of serious illness. Despite all this, modern life often treats sleep as a waste of time, something to be cut short for work or entertainment, and even worn as a badge of dedication. The science offers a blunt correction. Sleep is not the opposite of a productive life; it is one of its hidden foundations. To sleep well is not laziness, but one of the wisest things we can do for our minds and bodies alike.

questions.prompt: What does the sleeping brain actually do?
options: It sorts experiences and strengthens important memories.

questions.prompt: Why is sleep before an exam useful?
options: Because a rested brain can store information well.
Because it makes you forget everything.
Because it has no real effect.

questions.prompt: What is too little sleep linked to?
options: Poor concentration, weak immunity and higher illness risk.

questions.prompt: What does the writer say about sleep?
options: That it is a waste of time.
That it is not laziness but a hidden foundation of a productive life.
That we should sleep far less.
That sleep is only for children.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 整理经历、强化重要记忆)、细节(B 休息好的大脑更能存储信息)、细节(B 缺觉关联专注差、免疫弱、患病风险高)、主旨/态度(B 睡眠不是懒、而是高效生活的根基)。

sets.passage: The old town of Lijiang, with its narrow stone streets, wooden houses and flowing canals, has stood for more than eight hundred years. Today it faces a difficulty shared by historic places all over the world: how to welcome the modern world without losing the very character that makes it special. For centuries, Lijiang was a quiet town. Then its beauty made it famous, and tourists arrived in their millions. Tourism brought money and jobs, saving many old buildings from decay. But it also brought problems. Many original residents, finding their town overcrowded and expensive, moved away, replaced by shops and hotels selling souvenirs to visitors. Some worry that the town is slowly becoming a kind of theme park, beautiful but empty of real life. Protecting such a place is a delicate balance. Strict rules can preserve old buildings, but a town frozen in the past, with no living community, loses its soul. The challenge is to keep not only the stones but the spirit: the daily life, crafts and traditions that made the town what it is. There are no easy answers. Lijiang's story is a reminder that heritage is not just about buildings, but about people. To truly protect an old place, we must protect not only how it looks, but the living community that gives it meaning.

questions.prompt: What difficulty does Lijiang face?
options: Welcoming the modern world without losing its special character.
It has no tourists at all.
All its buildings are brand new.

questions.prompt: What problem did tourism bring to Lijiang?
options: Many original residents moved away, replaced by shops and hotels.
There was no money at all.

questions.prompt: What is the real challenge in protecting the town?
options: To keep not only the buildings but the living spirit and community.
To remove all the old houses.

questions.prompt: What does Lijiang's story remind us?
options: That heritage is about people, not just buildings.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 在迎接现代的同时保住特色)、细节(B 原住民迁走、被店铺旅馆取代)、细节(B 既留石头更要留精神与社区)、主旨(B 遗产关乎人、而不只是建筑)。

sets.passage: Few subjects cause as much worry today as the effect of artificial intelligence, or AI, on jobs. As machines learn to do more of what humans once did, from driving to writing, many fear a future of mass unemployment. The truth, as usual, is more complex than the fear suggests. History offers some comfort. Every major new technology, from the steam engine to the computer, has destroyed certain jobs while creating others, often ones no one could have imagined beforehand. Few of us today work as our great-grandparents did, yet there is no shortage of work. AI, optimists argue, will follow the same pattern, freeing people from dull or dangerous tasks to do more creative and human work. Yet there are reasons this time may be different. AI is spreading faster than past technologies and reaching into tasks once thought safely human, including many skilled professions. The danger is not so much that there will be no work, but that the change will be too rapid, leaving many people without the skills the new jobs require. The answer, most experts agree, lies not in trying to stop AI, which is impossible, but in preparing for it. This means rethinking education, helping workers learn new skills throughout their lives, and ensuring that the great wealth AI creates is shared widely. Handled wisely, AI could free us for better work; handled carelessly, it could leave many behind.

questions.prompt: What do many people fear about AI?
options: That it is far too expensive.
That it cannot work at all.

questions.prompt: What comfort does history offer?
options: That new technology destroys some jobs but creates new ones.

questions.prompt: Why might AI be different this time?
options: It is much slower than past technology.
It spreads faster and reaches skilled jobs, so change may be too rapid.
It creates no problems at all.

questions.prompt: What do most experts say is the answer?
options: To prepare for AI through education, retraining and sharing its wealth.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 担心大规模失业)、细节(B 新技术毁旧岗也造新岗)、细节(B 传播更快、触及技术岗、变化或太快)、主旨/推断(B 应对之道:教育、再培训、共享财富)。

sets.passage: When I first went to study abroad, I expected the hardest part to be the language. In fact, it was something subtler: the countless small differences in how people behaved that no dictionary could explain. In my new country, people stood closer when talking, looked me directly in the eye, and said exactly what they thought, sometimes in a way that, to me, seemed almost rude. At first, I often felt confused and even hurt, sure that I was being disliked. Slowly, I came to understand that these were simply different customs, not personal insults. What seemed rude directness to me was, to them, a sign of honesty and respect. What I considered polite — avoiding direct eye contact, softening my opinions — sometimes struck them as strange or even dishonest. Neither way was right or wrong; they were just different. This realisation changed me. I learned to be slower to judge and quicker to ask. I discovered that behind unfamiliar behaviour there is usually a logic that makes sense once you understand it. Most importantly, seeing my own customs through others' eyes made me realise how much of what I had thought was simply 'normal' was, in fact, just my own culture. Living between two cultures was not always comfortable, but it gave me a precious gift: the ability to see the world, and myself, from more than one point of view.

questions.prompt: What did the writer expect to be the hardest part?
How did people in the new country behave?
options: They stood close, made direct eye contact, and spoke their minds.

questions.prompt: What did the writer come to realise about the 'rude' behaviour?
options: That it was a different custom and a sign of honesty, not an insult.
That she should go straight home.
That the language was actually easy.

questions.prompt: What 'gift' did living between cultures give the writer?
options: The ability to see the world and herself from more than one point of view.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 以为语言最难)、细节(B 站得近、直视、直言)、推断(B 那是不同习俗、是真诚而非侮辱)、主旨(B 获得从多角度看世界与自我的能力)。
